content.full_text:
SHERIFF MICHAEL HENNESSEY
City and County of San Francisco • 333 City Hall • San Francisco 94102 • 554-7225

April 9, 1990


Mr. John Wallace
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California     95804

Dear John;

     Thank you for taking time to talk with me last week about
whether or not an elected official may use his campaign funds to
sponsor fundraising events and pay dues to political clubs and
community organizations.

     Sheriff Hennessey has recently been asked by the Affordable
Housing Alliance and the San Francisco County Democratic Central
Committee to sponsor their annual fundraising dinners. Elected
officials receive dozens of these requests throughout the year.
Typically, a sponsorship costs more than the regular ticket
price and entitles the sponsor to being listed on the event
program. We were concerned because many of both organizations
making such requests use the money raised to support candidates
for public office, a sponsorship paid for with campaign funds
might violate the Political Reform Act's prohibition against
transferring campaign funds from one candidate to another.

     As I understand our conversation, Sheriff Hennessey may use
campaign funds to pay for sponsorships so long as we indicate
that the contribution must be earmarked for non-contribution
purposes only. That is, the organization may use the Sheriff's
contribution to pay general operating costs, but may not use it
to support or oppose a candidate.

     The same rule would apply to annual dues to various
democratic clubs and community organizations.

     As we discussed, we will compose a short notice to be sent
with each sponsorship or dues payment explaining that the money
received from us must be earmarked.

     Thanks for your help.

Sincerely,


Eileen Hirst
Treasurer
Committee to Re-Elect Michael Hennessey
Friends of Michael Hennessey

(NOT PRINTED AT PUBLIC EXPENSE)

sections.question_synthetic: May an elected official use campaign funds to sponsor fundraising events and pay dues to political clubs and community organizations, particularly when those organizations use funds to support candidates?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming correspondence/follow-up letter from a requester, not an FPPC advice letter response. The document summarizes a prior verbal conversation with FPPC staff about campaign fund usage. No formal CONCLUSION or QUESTION sections present; structured as informal business letter. The actual FPPC response/advice was given verbally and is only referenced here.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: May an elected official use campaign funds to sponsor fundraising events and pay dues to political clubs and community organizations, particularly when those organizations use funds to support candidates?

Sheriff Hennessey has recently been asked by the Affordable Housing Alliance and the San Francisco County Democratic Central Committee to sponsor their annual fundraising dinners. Elected officials receive dozens of these requests throughout the year. Typically, a sponsorship costs more than the regular ticket price and entitles the sponsor to being listed on the event program. We were concerned because many of both organizations making such requests use the money raised to support candidates for public office, a sponsorship paid for with campaign funds might violate the Political Reform Act's prohibition against transferring campaign funds from one candidate to another. As I understand our conversation, Sheriff Hennessey may use campaign funds to pay for sponsorships so long as we indicate that the contribution must be earmarked for non-contribution purposes only. That is, the organization may use the Sheriff's contribution to pay general operating costs, but may not use it to support or oppose a candidate. embedding.summary: This is a follow-up letter from Sheriff Hennessey's treasurer to FPPC staff confirming the informal advice received regarding permissible uses of campaign funds for sponsorships and dues payments, with the requirement that contributions be earmarked for non-candidate support purposes.
